title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF MYCOTOXINS CONTAMINATION IN CASSAVA AND CASSAVA-BASED PRODUCTS CXC 82-2023

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF MYCOTOXINS CONTAMINATION IN CASSAVA AND CASSAVA-BASED PRODUCTS CXC 82-2023
sections.summary: Document title only: Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins Contamination in Cassava and Cassava‑Based Products (CXC 82-2023).

Code of practice for cassava and cassava-based products for human consumption providing guidance to national/local authorities, farmers, producers, manufacturers and distributors to prevent and reduce mycotoxin contamination through GAP, GMP, good storage practices (GSP) and good distribution practices (GDP).

sections.section: ## 3. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES APPLICABLE TO PRE-PLANTING STAGE
sections.summary: Pre-planting guidance covering site selection, land clearing/preparation and selection of cassava varieties and planting material to reduce fungal inocula, including advice on avoiding flood-prone areas, crop rotation, disposing of waste, tilling with clean tools and using healthy, disease-free cuttings.

Advise to avoid planting in valleys or flood-prone areas because water can transport fungal inocula, to plan crop rotation to reduce inocula (avoiding or managing rotation with susceptible crops such as groundnuts, maize and sugarcane) and to use low‑susceptibility crops where appropriate.

Land should be cleared with waste properly disposed to avoid contaminating cassava roots, soil loosened by tilling with clean/sanitized tools to reduce root stress during growth and maturation, and farmers are encouraged to adopt GAPs.
Select and plant healthy, pest- and disease-free cassava stems and prefer varieties with resistance to fungi and other pathogens, ensuring cuttings are free of fungal infection.

sections.section: ## 4. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES APPLICABLE TO PLANTING AND PRE-HARVEST STAGE
sections.summary: Planting and pre-harvest guidance to avoid using infected stems (use vertical planting where helpful), recommend pre-plant dipping of stakes in fungicide/insecticide/nutrient solutions, control weeds by manual/mechanical/herbicidal means, use approved pesticides per label and ensure irrigation is even while avoiding sprinkle irrigation during anthesis and root maturation to reduce fungal infection risk.

Do not plant stems showing rot; consider vertical planting to reduce rot during rainy seasons and dip planting stakes for five minutes in labeled prophylactic fungicide/insecticide/nutrient solutions, allow them to dry in shade before planting, and avoid planting on land recently cultivated with highly susceptible crops unless soil is free of A. flavus, A. parasiticus and related species.

Weed control guidance noting certain weeds can harbour toxigenic fungi and recommending manual, mechanical or approved herbicide use (post‑emergence when weeds appear, or pre‑emergence before planting), with caution to avoid mechanical injury and a recommendation that land preparation controls weeds for at least the first three months.
Approved pesticides may be used to minimize insect damage and fungal infection, with planning aided by weather models and mandatory adherence to all label instructions and authorization requirements.

Irrigation should be applied evenly to reduce plant stress and ensure adequate water supply, but sprinkle irrigation during anthesis and root maturation should be avoided because excess moisture favors fungal infection.

sections.section: 5. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES APPLICABLE TO HARVEST STAGE
sections.summary: Harvest-stage guidance covering planned harvesting to maintain quality, use of cleaned/sanitized conveyances to prevent mechanical damage, and holding/storage recommendations to avoid sun, heat and mechanical damage with an ideal progression to processing within 2–3 days (or use enhanced storage) and noting enhanced methods can extend fresh root shelf life by 2–6 weeks.

Harvest planning should determine amounts to meet market demand and harvest when soil is slightly soft (not saturated) to facilitate soil removal and reduce fungal contamination, with measures to prevent damage when harvesting in difficult conditions.
Containers and conveyances used to collect and transport harvested roots must prevent mechanical damage and be cleaned, sanitized and dried.

Prior to processing, keep cassava roots out of sun, high temperatures and mechanical damage because of high water activity; plan a continuous flow to final product with an ideal unenhanced holding time of 2–3 days, while enhanced storage (low temperature plus fungicide treatment or waxing) can extend fresh root shelf life by 2–6 weeks for large‑scale storage or export.

sections.section: 6. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES APPLICABLE TO POST-HARVEST STAGES
sections.summary: Post-harvest guidance for cassava and cassava-based products outlines processing steps that may affect fungal contamination and refers to CXC 73-2013, with a key recommendation to initiate processing within 8–12 hours of receiving roots to avoid spoilage.

Cassava can be processed into diverse fermented and non‑fermented products and processing should begin within 8–12 hours of receipt; washing, peeling and immediate boiling/steaming (for sweet varieties) are recommended to reduce fungal inocula and inactivate fungi.
If cassava roots are to be processed immediately they should be washed to remove surface dirt and reduce toxigenic fungal inocula, using potable or appropriately treated water.
Peeled cassava roots should be processed immediately after washing, and peeling must be done in a clean environment (not where other crops are stored) to avoid contamination.
For sweet cassava varieties, boil or steam immediately after peeling and washing to inactivate fungi, and if not used immediately take measures to prevent fungal re‑contamination.

sections.section: 6.2 Size reduction: Grating, pulping and slicing or chipping
sections.summary: During size‑reduction (grating, pulping, slicing/chipping) ensure practices and equipment prevent fungal contamination, dry chips/slices on clean raised platforms or use well‑maintained artificial dryers to reach acceptable moisture content, and keep all processing environments and tools clean.

Describes fermentation of cassava roots to further eliminate cyanide, develop flavour and stabilize product; requires that containers and equipment remain clean to avoid becoming sources of inoculum and notes typical fermentation duration of 2–5 days.

Requires monitoring moisture of cassava-based products (notably flours and granules) before packaging to prevent microbial growth, mandates use of food‑grade, non‑moisture‑absorbing packaging materials, and allows vacuum or modified‑atmosphere packaging where necessary.

Sets transport sanitation and protection requirements: transport containers must be dry, free of old crop dust, visible fungal growth, musty odour, insects and contaminants and be cleaned/disinfected with non‑contaminating agents; shipments must be protected from moisture and temperature fluctuations to avoid condensation and mycotoxin formation, and pest‑proof containers should be used.

Requires specific, legible storage instructions on packaging (for storage before and after opening) in clear language to protect cassava and cassava‑based products from conditions that promote fungal growth and contamination (e.g., 'store in a cool, dry, well‑ventilated area').
